name: Sun
oneLine: Our nearest star — a middle-aged G-type dwarf that drives all life and weather on Earth.
whyItMatters: The Sun fuses about 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium every second, releasing the energy that powers the biosphere. Its activity cycles affect GPS accuracy, satellite drag, and power grids, making solar awareness genuinely practical.
observationTips: Never look at the Sun with naked eyes or unfiltered optics — permanent eye damage can occur in seconds.
Use certified solar filters (ISO 12312-2) or a pinhole projector to observe sunspots safely.
At sunset or sunrise the orange disc is safe to glance at briefly, but sunspot detail requires a proper filter.
kidFriendlySummary: The Sun is a giant ball of super-hot gas sitting at the centre of our Solar System. It's so big that about a million Earths could fit inside it! Its light and warmth travel 150 million kilometres to reach us in just over 8 minutes.
sourceNotes: NASA Solar Facts; ESA/SOHO mission data; IAU 2012 nominal solar constants.

name: Moon
oneLine: Earth's only natural satellite and the most closely studied body beyond our planet.
whyItMatters: The Moon stabilises Earth's axial tilt, moderates our seasons, and drives tides. It is the only celestial body beyond Earth visited by humans, and remains the most accessible target for future crewed exploration.
observationTips: The terminator (shadow boundary) is the best place to study craters — low-angle sunlight makes relief dramatic.
Binoculars reveal major maria (dark lava plains) and mountain ranges clearly.
New Moon evenings are ideal for deep-sky observing because the sky is darkest.
kidFriendlySummary: The Moon is Earth's rocky neighbour in space. It has no air and is covered in craters from asteroid impacts. Every night it looks slightly different because of how sunlight hits it as it orbits us.
sourceNotes: NASA Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter data; IAU Lunar Nomenclature; Apollo mission records.

name: Mercury
oneLine: The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun — a world of extreme temperature swings.
whyItMatters: Mercury's unusually large iron core and proximity to the Sun make it a natural laboratory for testing theories of planetary formation and general relativity (its orbital precession confirmed Einstein's predictions).
observationTips: Mercury is never far from the Sun — look low in the west just after sunset or low in the east before sunrise.
It is best seen around greatest elongation (its maximum angular distance from the Sun).
Binoculars help, but avoid using them when the Sun is above the horizon.
kidFriendlySummary: Mercury is the smallest planet in our Solar System and zips around the Sun faster than any other planet. Because it's so close to the Sun it gets super-hot during the day and freezing cold at night.
sourceNotes: NASA MESSENGER mission; JPL Solar System Exploration fact sheets.

name: Venus
oneLine: Earth's twin in size but a hellish world of thick acid clouds and crushing pressure.
whyItMatters: Venus is the brightest natural object in the night sky after the Moon. Its runaway greenhouse effect makes it the prime cautionary case study for climate science. Ancient navigators called it the 'Morning Star' and 'Evening Star'.
observationTips: Venus is brilliant enough to cast faint shadows — look for them on snow or white surfaces on clear nights.
It shows phases like the Moon when viewed through binoculars or a small telescope.
Best observed in twilight when the contrast between Venus and sky is comfortable for the eyes.
kidFriendlySummary: Venus is the brightest 'star' you can see in the sky, but it's actually a planet! It's about the same size as Earth, but its thick, toxic atmosphere traps so much heat that it's even hotter than Mercury.
sourceNotes: NASA Magellan mission; ESA Venus Express; JPL planetary fact sheets.

name: Mars
oneLine: The Red Planet — a rusty desert world with the tallest volcano and longest canyon in the Solar System.
whyItMatters: Mars is the primary near-term target for human exploration. Evidence of ancient river valleys and lake beds suggests it once had liquid water, raising questions about past — and possibly present — microbial life.
observationTips: Mars is distinctly reddish — easy to identify among the stars.
Its brightness varies enormously: at opposition it can rival Jupiter; at its faintest it looks like a dim reddish star.
A small telescope at opposition shows the polar ice cap and dark surface markings.
kidFriendlySummary: Mars is called the Red Planet because of the rusty dust that covers it. It has the biggest volcano in the Solar System — Olympus Mons is three times taller than Mount Everest! Robots from Earth are exploring it right now.
sourceNotes: NASA Mars Exploration Program; ESA Mars Express; MRO and Perseverance rover data.

name: Jupiter
oneLine: The Solar System's largest planet — a gas giant with iconic storms and 95 known moons.
whyItMatters: Jupiter's gravity shapes the entire Solar System: it catapults comets and asteroids outward, acting as a 'cosmic shield.' Its moon Europa harbours a subsurface ocean considered one of the best candidates for extraterrestrial life.

quickFacts.label: Great Red Spot
quickFacts.value: Storm > 150 years old, wider than Earth

observationTips: Jupiter is the brightest object in the night sky after the Moon and Venus — impossible to miss.
Even 7×50 binoculars show the four Galilean moons as pinpoints of light.
A small telescope reveals the cloud bands and occasionally the shadow transits of its moons.
kidFriendlySummary: Jupiter is a massive ball of gas — so big that all the other planets could fit inside it! It has a giant storm called the Great Red Spot that has been swirling for hundreds of years. Its moon Europa might even have an ocean hidden under ice.
sourceNotes: NASA Juno mission; Galileo spacecraft data; IAU Minor Planet Center moon catalogue.

name: Saturn
oneLine: The ringed jewel of the Solar System — a gas giant whose iconic rings are made of ice and rock.
whyItMatters: Saturn's rings — spanning ~270,000 km but only ~10–100 m thick — are the most spectacular structure visible from a backyard telescope. Its moon Titan has a thick atmosphere and lakes of liquid methane, making it a key target for astrobiology.
observationTips: Saturn's rings are visible in any telescope above ~25×; at 50× they are a jaw-dropping sight.
The rings' tilt relative to Earth changes on a ~15-year cycle — the view varies significantly year to year.
Look for a yellowish, steady 'star' that does not twinkle — that is Saturn.
kidFriendlySummary: Saturn is famous for its beautiful rings made of billions of chunks of ice and rock. It's so light that it could float in water! Saturn has over 140 moons — more than any other planet.
sourceNotes: NASA Cassini mission; IAU moon catalogue; JPL Saturn fact sheet.

name: Uranus
oneLine: An ice giant that rolls on its side — its extreme axial tilt gives it 42-year-long seasons.
whyItMatters: Uranus is the only planet whose axis is tilted past 90°, meaning it orbits the Sun essentially on its side. Understanding this tilt and its faint ring system informs theories of giant impacts during Solar System formation.
observationTips: Uranus is just at the limit of naked-eye visibility on very dark nights (magnitude ~5.7).
Binoculars show it as a blue-green 'star' — its colour comes from methane in the atmosphere.
A telescope of 150 mm or more may show its disc — it appears as a featureless greenish dot.
kidFriendlySummary: Uranus is a giant blue-green planet made mostly of icy materials — scientists call it an 'ice giant.' It's unique because it spins on its side, like a rolling ball, instead of spinning upright like most other planets.
sourceNotes: NASA Voyager 2 flyby data; Keck Observatory ring measurements; HST imaging.

name: Neptune
oneLine: The windiest planet — a deep-blue ice giant with supersonic storms and 14 known moons.
whyItMatters: Neptune was predicted mathematically before it was ever observed — the first planet discovered through theoretical calculation. Its largest moon Triton orbits backwards, suggesting it was captured from the Kuiper Belt.
observationTips: Neptune requires binoculars or a telescope — it appears as a tiny blue dot and does not resolve in small scopes.
A finder chart is essential; Neptune moves slowly enough that it stays near the same stars for weeks.
At magnitude ~7.8, it is too faint for naked-eye observation from most sites.
kidFriendlySummary: Neptune is the farthest planet from the Sun and has the strongest winds in the Solar System — so fast they could blow a plane apart! It's so far away that it takes 165 years to go once around the Sun.
sourceNotes: NASA Voyager 2 flyby (1989); HST Neptune observations; JPL planetary fact sheets.

name: ISS
oneLine: The International Space Station — a habitable laboratory orbiting Earth at ~400 km altitude.
whyItMatters: The ISS is the largest structure ever assembled in space and has been continuously inhabited since November 2000. It serves as a testbed for long-duration spaceflight, micro-gravity science, and international cooperation among 15 nations.
observationTips: The ISS is easily the brightest satellite — it can outshine Venus (up to magnitude −4).
It is visible as a steady white dot moving quickly across the sky in 2–5 minutes.
Use the NASA 'Spot the Station' app or Heavens-Above.com to find the next pass for your location.
kidFriendlySummary: The ISS is like a giant science lab floating in space where astronauts live and work. It's about as long as a football field and travels so fast that it goes around the whole Earth 16 times every day! You can see it with your own eyes on a clear night.
sourceNotes: NASA ISS program; ESA Human Spaceflight; CelesTrak TLE data.

name: Sirius
oneLine: The brightest star in the night sky — a blue-white star just 8.6 light-years away.
whyItMatters: Ancient Egyptians used Sirius's heliacal rising (first appearance before sunrise after months of invisibility) to predict the annual Nile floods. It is in the constellation Canis Major and has a faint white-dwarf companion, Sirius B.
observationTips: Sirius scintillates (twinkles) dramatically near the horizon due to atmospheric refraction — blue and red flashes are normal.
Follow Orion's Belt southward to find Sirius reliably.
At southern latitudes it passes nearly overhead; from northern latitudes it stays low, enhancing the twinkling effect.
kidFriendlySummary: Sirius is the brightest star in the whole night sky! It's a bit bigger and much hotter than our Sun, and it's so close (in space terms) that its light only takes about 8 and a half years to reach us.
sourceNotes: Hipparcos parallax catalogue; SIMBAD astronomical database; Gaia DR3.

name: Polaris
oneLine: The North Star — a Cepheid variable that marks true north to within less than a degree.
whyItMatters: For millennia Polaris has been the navigator's star, sitting within ~0.7° of the celestial north pole. As a Cepheid variable with a period of ~4 days, it is also a calibration standard for measuring cosmic distances.
observationTips: Find the two 'pointer stars' at the end of the Big Dipper's bowl — they point directly to Polaris.
Polaris marks north: its altitude equals your latitude, so it doubles as a rough latitude finder.
It is not the brightest star — Sirius is about 6× brighter — but its fixed position makes it unique.
kidFriendlySummary: Polaris, the North Star, is special because it stays almost perfectly still in the sky while all other stars seem to move around it as Earth rotates. For thousands of years, explorers and sailors have used it to figure out which way is north.
sourceNotes: Gaia DR3 parallax data; IAU Cepheid period-luminosity calibration; precession calculations.

name: Orion
oneLine: The Hunter — one of the most recognisable constellations, home to the Great Nebula and two supergiant stars.
whyItMatters: Orion is a universal constellation appearing in the myths of nearly every ancient culture. The Orion Nebula (M42) is the nearest massive star-forming region to Earth and is visible to the naked eye. Betelgeuse, a red supergiant, is a candidate for a nearby supernova.

quickFacts.label: Key stars
quickFacts.value: Betelgeuse (red), Rigel (blue-white), Bellatrix, Mintaka, Alnilam, Alnitak

quickFacts.label: Orion Nebula (M42)
quickFacts.value: ~1,344 light-years away, visible to naked eye

quickFacts.label: Visibility
quickFacts.value: Visible from most of Earth, December–March evenings

observationTips: Orion's Belt — three evenly spaced stars in a line — is the easiest star pattern to find in the winter sky.
Look below the Belt for Orion's Sword: the fuzzy middle 'star' is the Orion Nebula, visible to the naked eye.
Binoculars dramatically enhance the nebula and reveal its core cluster of young stars (the Trapezium).
kidFriendlySummary: Orion the Hunter is one of the most famous star patterns in the sky. Its three belt stars are perfectly lined up, which makes it easy to spot. Hidden in Orion's sword is a beautiful cloud of gas and dust called the Orion Nebula, where new stars are being born right now!
sourceNotes: IAU constellation boundaries; NASA/ESA Hubble M42 imagery; SIMBAD star catalogue.

name: Pleiades
oneLine: The Seven Sisters — a sparkling open cluster of hot blue stars about 440 light-years away.
whyItMatters: The Pleiades appear in the mythology of cultures on every inhabited continent — from Greek mythology to Māori wayfinding to Aboriginal Australian sky lore. Their heliacal rising historically marked the start of the sailing season in the Mediterranean.
observationTips: On a dark night you can count 6 to 9 Pleiades stars with the naked eye — averted vision helps.
Binoculars reveal dozens of stars and a faint blue reflection nebula around the brightest members.
Follow Orion's Belt northeast to find the V-shaped Hyades cluster, then continue to the Pleiades.
kidFriendlySummary: The Pleiades, or Seven Sisters, look like a tiny fuzzy patch in the sky. With binoculars you can see they're actually a beautiful cluster of bright blue stars. Almost every ancient culture in the world had a story about these stars — they're that famous!
sourceNotes: Gaia DR3 cluster membership; Hipparcos parallax measurements; IAU star names.
